title: How to Use Intuition When Making Decisions About Your Finances
meta_description: Trust your gut with money. It knows what your spreadsheet misses. Stop ignoring the tight chest and quiet warnings. Intuition plus logic creates financial decisions you can actually stand behind.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Making Decisions About Your Finances
og_description: Trust your gut with money. It knows what your spreadsheet misses. Stop ignoring the tight chest and quiet warnings. Intuition plus logic creates financial decisions you can actually stand behind.
body:
Your Gut Knows Your Bank Account Better Than Your Spreadsheet Does

Let me guess. You've made financial decisions that looked perfect on paper but felt wrong in your bones. And you made them anyway. Because that's what responsible adults do, right? They ignore the tight chest, the knot in the stomach, the quiet voice saying "don't." They run the numbers, make the logical choice, and then wonder why they feel sick about it.

You're not bad with money. You're not impulsive or irresponsible. You're someone whose intuition is screaming at you, and you've been trained to turn down the volume. But here's the thing - your intuition isn't your enemy when it comes to finances. It's your most underused asset. And learning to use it doesn't mean throwing your budget out the window. It means making decisions that your whole self can stand behind, not just the part that crunches numbers.

Why Your Financial Intuition Got Shut Down

Think back. When were you first told that your feelings about money didn't matter? Maybe it was when you wanted to spend your allowance on something silly and your parents said "that's a waste." Maybe it was when you had a bad feeling about a purchase but couldn't explain why, so you did it anyway and regretted it. Maybe it was every time someone said "be practical" when what they really meant was "stop listening to yourself."

Most of us were raised in a culture that treats money like pure math. Numbers are real, feelings are suspect. Logic is trustworthy, intuition is woo-woo. But here's the problem - your nervous system picks up information your conscious mind misses. It reads the energy of a salesperson, the subtle tension in a financial advisor's voice, the way your body relaxes or contracts when you look at a price tag. That's not irrational. That's data. Real, usable data that your spreadsheet will never capture.

So when you feel that pull toward a purchase that doesn't make sense, or that resistance to a "smart" investment, you're not being difficult. You're receiving information. The question is whether you're willing to listen.

The Difference Between Intuition and Fear

This is where people get tripped up. "But Kalesh, how do I know it's intuition and not just anxiety?" Fair question. Here's the shortcut - intuition feels clear, steady, and neutral. Fear feels tight, urgent, and agitated. Intuition says "not this one" without panic. Fear says "what if everything falls apart" with a racing heart.

Intuition doesn't need to convince you. It just knows. Fear needs you to spiral. Does that land? Try this - when you're facing a financial decision, pause and check your body. Is your chest tight? Are your shoulders up by your ears? That's fear. Is there a quiet certainty, even if you can't explain why? That's intuition. One is a signal from your survival brain. The other is a signal from your whole system.

And here's a hard truth - sometimes they overlap. You can be intuitively sure a job isn't right for you and also terrified to leave the paycheck. The intuition doesn't remove the fear. It just gives you a compass when the fear is screaming. You learn to act on the compass while the fear is still yelling in the back seat.

How to Actually Use Intuition With Money (Practical Steps)

Let's get concrete. You don't need to meditate for an hour before every purchase. You need simple, repeatable practices that honor both your logic and your gut. Here's what works:

1. The 24-Hour Rule for Every Non-Emergency Decision

If it's not rent, food, or a medical bill, wait 24 hours. Not forever. Just one day. During that time, don't research. Don't ask friends. Don't make pros and cons lists. Just sit with the decision and notice what happens in your body. Do you feel relief when you think about not buying it? Do you feel a quiet pull toward it? Does the thought of it drain you or energize you?

Your logical mind wants to solve the problem immediately. Your intuition needs space to breathe. Give it that space. You'll be amazed how many "great deals" feel wrong after a good night's sleep.

2. Check Your Body Before You Check Your Budget

Before you open your banking app or look at a price tag, take three slow breaths and scan your body. Where do you feel tension? Where do you feel open? Then look at the numbers. Notice what shifts. If your shoulders drop when you see the total, that's a green light. If your stomach clenches, that's a yellow flag, even if the math works.

This isn't about ignoring the math. It's about adding another layer of information. You can still make the purchase if the math says yes and your body says no - but at least you'll know you're overriding a signal. And that awareness alone will save you from the kind of regret that comes from pretending you didn't feel it.

3. Ask Better Questions

Instead of "Can I afford this?" ask "Does this align with where I'm going?" Instead of "Is this a good investment?" ask "Does this feel like a yes or a no in my whole body?" Instead of "What's the smart choice?" ask "What choice will I be grateful I made a year from now?"

Your intuition responds to open, curious questions. It shuts down under pressure and judgment. So soften the questions. Let them be exploratory. You're not looking for a perfect answer. You're looking for direction.

"Your intuition isn't trying to replace your logic. It's trying to complete it. The numbers tell you what's possible. Your gut tells you what's right for you. Both are necessary. Neither is sufficient alone."

When Your Intuition Says No to a "Smart" Financial Move

This is the hard one. You've done the research. Everyone says it's a good idea. The numbers check out. But something in you is saying no. What do you do?

First, stop gaslighting yourself. You're not being irrational. You're picking up on something the numbers don't show. Maybe it's the wrong timing. Maybe it's the wrong person to work with. Maybe it's a good opportunity but not your opportunity. Your intuition doesn't always give you the reason - it just gives you the signal. Trust the signal, even without the explanation.

Second, give yourself permission to wait. You don't have to decide right now. "Not yet" is a complete sentence. If it's truly a good opportunity, it will still be there in a month. If it's not, you just saved yourself from forcing something that wasn't meant for you. Right?!

Third, ask your intuition for more information. Not in a demanding way, but in a curious way. "What are you protecting me from?" "What am I not seeing?" "What would make this a yes?" Sometimes the answer comes immediately. Sometimes it takes days. But the question opens a channel that the fear-based mind can't access.

I've had clients who turned down "perfect" job offers because something felt off, only to discover later the company was about to restructure. I've had clients who passed on "smart" investments that turned out to be scams. Their intuition knew before their logic could prove it. Yours does too.

The Role of Money Wounds in Financial Intuition

Here's where it gets layered. Your intuition about money isn't clean. It's mixed up with your history. If you grew up with scarcity, your gut might say "no" to every opportunity because survival mode is always on. If you grew up with neglect, your gut might say "yes" to things that fill a void but drain your bank account. If you grew up watching your parents fight about money, your whole nervous system might shut down when you look at a bill.

This doesn't mean your intuition is broken. It means you need to learn the difference between your intuitive signal and your conditioned response. And the only way to do that is practice. Start small. Use your intuition for a $20 purchase. Then a $50 one. Then a $100 one. Build the muscle before you trust it with big decisions.

A book that helped me untangle this is The Gifts of Imperfection by Brené Brown. It's not a money book, but it's a book about showing up as your whole self - including your messy, conditioned, healing self. And that's exactly what you need to bring to your financial decisions. See on Amazon (paid link)

Practical Exercises to Strengthen Financial Intuition

You don't learn this by reading. You learn by doing. Here are three exercises to start today:

Exercise 1: The Penny Test

Pick up a penny. Hold it in your hand. Close your eyes and ask: "Is this money safe for me?" Notice what your body does. Then ask: "Is this money a yes or a no?" Don't think. Just feel. The answer might come as a sensation, a word, a color, or a knowing. Trust whatever comes. Then do the same with a larger bill. Notice the difference. This trains your system to read the energy of money itself, not just your thoughts about it.

Exercise 2: The Receipt Review

Look at your last month's spending. For each purchase, close your eyes and remember how you felt when you made it. Which purchases still feel good? Which ones feel heavy or regretful? Don't judge yourself. Just notice the pattern. Your intuition is showing you where you were aligned and where you were overriding. Use that information going forward.

Exercise 3: The Future Self Check

Before any financial decision, close your eyes and imagine yourself one year from now. Ask that version of you: "Did this choice serve you?" Notice what comes. It might be a feeling, a word, or an image. Your future self has no stake in the drama of right now. They just know what worked. Listen to them.

Another resource that helped me understand how to work with this kind of layered intuition is The Power of Now by Eckhart Tolle. It's about getting out of your thinking mind and into your present-moment awareness - which is exactly where your intuition lives. See on Amazon (paid link)

What to Do When You Get It Wrong

You will. I have. Everyone has. You'll follow your gut and it'll lead you to a decision that doesn't work out. That doesn't mean your intuition is broken. It means you're learning the language. Think of it like learning a new instrument. You're going to hit wrong notes. That's how you learn which ones are right.

When you get it wrong, don't beat yourself up. Don't swear off intuition forever. Instead, ask: "What did I learn about how my intuition speaks to me?" "Was I mistaking fear for intuition?" "Was I forcing a yes when there was a no?" "What would I do differently next time?"

The goal isn't perfect decisions. The goal is a stronger connection to yourself. Every wrong turn teaches you something about how your system works. And that knowledge compounds. Over time, you get faster, clearer, and more trusting of the process.

When Logic and Intuition Agree

This is the sweet spot. The place where the numbers feel good and your body feels good. Where the spreadsheet and the gut are pointing in the same direction. That's when you move with confidence. That's when you know you're not just making a smart choice - you're making your choice.

But here's the catch - you can't get to that alignment if you've been ignoring one side. If you've been all logic, all the time, your intuition will be weak and unreliable when you finally need it. If you've been all intuition, no spreadsheets, you'll make choices that feel right but leave you in a mess. The integration is the point. Both channels. Both valid. Both necessary.

One of the best books I've found on this kind of whole-self decision-making is Designing Your Life by Bill Burnett and Dave Evans. It's about using both intuition and experimentation to build a life that works for you - financially and otherwise. See on Amazon (paid link)

Your Financial Intuition Is a Relationship, Not a Tool

You don't pick up a hammer and expect it to talk to you. But your intuition isn't a tool. It's a relationship. It's the part of you that knows what you need before you can articulate it. It's the part that sees around corners. It's the part that has always been there, even when you were told to shut it up.

This relationship needs tending. It needs quiet. It needs trust. It needs you to show up consistently, not just when you're desperate. It needs you to make small decisions with it so it's ready when the big ones come. Know what I mean?

Start today. Pick one small financial decision - a purchase, a subscription, a donation - and check in with your body before you decide. Don't force anything. Just notice. Let intuition have a seat at the table. It's been waiting for an invitation.

And if you want to go deeper into this work - understanding your money wounds, your intuitive style, and how to bring them together - I've put together a free assessment that walks you through exactly that. Take the free assessment here to see where your intuition is strongest and where it needs support.
